faqs.a: Doomscrolling is the habit of compulsively scrolling through negative news and social media and finding it hard to stop, even when it makes you feel worse. It is a popular term, not a clinical diagnosis, and the compulsive loop is what defines it.

faqs.q: Why is doomscrolling so hard to stop?
faqs.a: Feeds are built with endless scroll and attention-grabbing content, and the brain treats threatening information as urgent, so checking feels productive even when it deepens unease. There is often a pull that the next post will settle the anxiety, and a let-down when it does not.

faqs.q: How can I cut back on doomscrolling?
faqs.a: Simple steps like setting time limits, turning off alerts, and keeping screen-free routines around sleep help many people. If persistent anxiety or low mood is fueling the habit, talking with a mental health professional can address what is driving the pull.
